title-group.article-title: Insomnia and stroke risk: A systematic review with meta-analysis of analytical observational studies and mendelian randomization studies

contrib-group.aff: aGrupo de Investigación Neurociencias, Metabolismo, Efectividad Clínica y Sanitaria (NEMECS), Universidad Científica del Sur, Lima, 15001, Peru
bFacultad de Ciencias de la Salud, Universidad Privada de Tacna, Tacna, 23000, Peru
cSociedad Cientifica de San Fernando, Lima, 15001, Peru
dFacultad de Medicina de San Fernando, Universidad Nacional Mayor de San Marcos, Lima, 15001, Peru

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Panamericana Sur km 19, Lima, 42, Peru. mcabanillasl@cientifica.edu.pe

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: Stroke is one of the leading causes of death worldwide, with ischemic stroke being the most frequent subtype. Factors such as sleep disorders may increase the risk of stroke; however, solid evidence is lacking. Therefore, the objective was to synthesize current evidence on the relationship between insomnia and its role as a risk factor for stroke. A systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted, with searches performed in PubMed, Embase, Scopus, Google Scholar, and Web of Science up to March 2024. The included studies were observational or Mendelian randomized studies that evaluated the diagnosis or symptoms of insomnia and any type of stroke as an outcome. The study selection process, data extraction, and risk of bias assessment were conducted in pairs. A random-effects meta-analysis was performed to estimate the effect of the outcome. Trial Registration: PROSPERO CRD42024519745. A total of 17 studies (12 observational studies and 5 Mendelian randomization studies) were analyzed, representing a total population of 7,696,920 patients. In the pooled analysis, no significant association was observed between the risk of developing any type of stroke and the presence of any insomnia symptom (HR: 1.16; 95% CI: 0.88–1.52; I2 = 60%), but a higher risk of developing ischemic stroke was found (HR: 1.07; 95% CI: 1.04–1.10; I2 = 60%) with a high level of evidence certainty. Conversely, an increased risk of any type of stroke was observed when insomnia was diagnosed (HR: 1.45; 95% CI: 1.32–1.61).

list-item.p: First systematic review integrating observational and Mendelian randomization studies.
Distinguishes insomnia symptoms from clinically diagnosed insomnia in stroke risk.
Insomnia symptoms are associated with increased risk of ischemic stroke.
Diagnosed insomnia shows stronger associations with all stroke subtypes.
Findings support biological plausibility and guide future objective sleep research.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Stroke affects approximately 15 million people worldwide, with ischemic stroke being the most common subtype (80%) [1]. It is the second leading cause of death globally, with an annual mortality rate of 5 million people [2]. Among the survivors of a stroke, half suffer from chronic disabilities, making this condition the second leading cause of disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) in individuals over 50 years old [3]. Additionally, the estimated global cost of stroke exceeds 721 billion US dollars (0.66% of the global GDP), making it a major public health issue, particularly in low- and middle-income countries [4].

There are common factors that increase the likelihood of suffering a stroke, whether ischemic or hemorrhagic, like obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), hypertension, diabetes mellitus, obesity, heart disease, smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, hypercholesterolemia, and hypertriglyceridemia [5]. Non-modifiable factors such as sex and age also influence the incidence and mortality of stroke, being more frequent in young men but more lethal in older women [6]. In general, sleep disorders are considered risk factors, but non-apneic sleep disorders, such as insomnia, have less solid evidence [7].

Insomnia is a sleep disorder characterized by difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, accompanied by daytime impairments such as fatigue, reduced concentration, and excessive concern about sleep quality [8]. Sleep difficulties or symptoms of insomnia are among the most common medical complaints, affecting nearly one-third of the adult population. Additionally, insomnia is known to be a persistent disorder, lasting more than five years in over 40% of patients with severe symptoms at the time of diagnosis [9]. The mechanisms by which sleep disorders increase the risk of stroke are not yet fully understood. One pathophysiological mechanism proposed involves inflammation [10]. C-reactive protein, a non-specific marker of acute inflammation, is released by hepatocytes in response to interleukin-6 expression [11] and it has been suggested that inflammation may contribute to atherosclerosis, thrombosis, and small vessel diseases in the brain, increasing the risk of various types of strokes [11,12]. Furthermore, the link between insomnia and elevated inflammatory biomarkers has been supported by multiple studies [13].

Although evidence suggests that stroke survivors are at increased risk of insomnia, the association between insomnia as a risk factor for stroke has only been partially assessed in three systematic reviews [[14], [15], [16]]. Notably, two of these reviews conducted a meta-analysis, which, however, focused on cardiovascular disease without a specific analysis of stroke or its subtypes. Therefore, our objective was to synthesize current evidence on the relationship between the diagnosis of insomnia and its role as a risk factor for stroke.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review was reported according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) [17]. The study protocol was registered in PROSPERO with code CRD42024519745.

sec.title: PECO question
sec.p: Our research question was: What is the magnitude of the association of the diagnosis or symptoms of insomnia with the risk of stroke?

sec.title: Data sources
sec.p: We searched in PubMed, Embase, Scopus, Google Scholar, Web of Science until March 2024. The search strategy for PubMed was adapted for use in the other databases (Supplementary Material S1). There were no restrictions on language or publication dates. We completed the search by reviewing the bibliographic references of the studies included and selecting the articles that met the requirements of this review.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Studies were included if they met the following criteria: 1) The inclusion of adult participants (aged >18 years old); 2) Studies evaluating the diagnosis or symptoms of insomnia (early morning awakening, difficulties in initiating or maintaining sleep, and daytime dysfunction) as exposure variable; 3) Studies evaluating any type of stroke as an outcome; 4) Analytical observational studies or Mendelian randomization studies. We excluded narrative and systematic reviews, studies in non-humans, case reports, conference abstracts and letters.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: The electronic search results were imported into the Endnote X9, then duplicate records were exported to Rayyan (https://rayyan.qcri.org/). A peer review process was performed by two reviewers (GTS and ASM) and any discrepancies were resolved by consensus and with the opinion of a third author (MCL). These reviewers assessed inclusion criteria independently by reading the full texts of the potentially relevant studies selected and discrepancies were resolved by consensus. The complete list of excluded articles is provided in Supplementary Material S2.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Two authors (MPG and ASM) performed data extraction using a data extraction form, and disagreements were resolved by a third author (MCL). We extracted the following information: title of the study, first author, year of publication, study design, country where the study was performed, number of participants, sex, age, sample time, crude and adjusted association measures, and type of stroke. If additional data were needed, we contacted the corresponding author by email to request further information.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: The quality of the studies was assessed with the Newcastle Ottawa Scale (NOS) by two authors (GTS and ASM) [18]. This tool evaluates the quality of published non-randomized studies and is based on three items: selection, comparability, and outcome/exposure. Each item has sub-items, on which a star-based score was assigned. Studies with scores ≥6 were considered as having a low risk of bias (high quality), with scores of 4-5 having a moderate risk of bias, and scores <4 were considered to have a high risk of bias. In addition, the Mendelian randomization studies were evaluated with the tool developed by Treur et al. [19], which takes into account the domains of phenotype measurement, instrument strength and the bidirectional effects tested. A study was considered to have low risk if all domains were rated as “+”, while any domain rated as “−/+” was considered as having moderate risk and “-” was classified as high risk of bias.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: A random-effects meta-analysis using the Paule-Mandel estimator was conducted to calculate the pooled effect of the outcome. When pooling was not feasible, a narrative synthesis was provided. Hazard ratios (HR) and odds ratios (OR), with their corresponding 95% confidence intervals (95% CI), were aggregated based on stroke type and association measure without further conversion. Study heterogeneity was evaluated using the I2 statistic, with heterogeneity categorized as low (I2 < 30%), moderate (I2 = 30–60%), and high (I2 > 60%). The metabin function of the meta package in R version 4.1.0 (www.r-project.org) was employed for analysis. A leave-one-out sensitivity analysis was also performed to assess the robustness of the findings.

sec.title: Certainty of evidence assessment
sec.p: Two authors (MCL and MPG) independently assessed the certainty of our pooled results and qualitative synthesis applying the Grading of Recommendation, Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) to dichotomous outcomes [20], and narrative synthesis [21], respectively. This assessment is based on five domains: study limitations (risk of bias of the studies included), imprecision (sample size and confidence interval), indirectness (generalizability), inconsistency (heterogeneity), and publication bias. The certainty of the evidence was characterized as high, moderate, low, or very low.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: We identified 5356 studies through our systematic search. After removing duplicates, we screened 3517 studies and finally included 17 (Supplementary Material S3).

sec.title: Characteristics of the studies included
sec.p:
We included 12 analytical observational studies (8 cohorts, 3 cross-sectionals, 1 case-control) and 5 mendelian randomization studies. These studies included a total of 7,696,920 patients with an average range in age of between 30.9 and 69.37 years. There was heterogeneity among the characteristics of insomnia, but the outcome in most cases was ischemic stroke. The remaining characteristics are summarized in Table 1.Table 1
Characteristics of the studies included evaluating the risk of stroke in patients with insomnia (n = 17).Table 1
Study-IDCountryStudy designSampleGender (%) and Age (years) mean (SD)Measurement of symptoms insomniaClassification of insomniaType of stroke (%)Follow-up time

Age: 55-69a

Age: 56.5 (8.1)

Age: 48.23 (13.19)

DSM: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders; LIS: Lebanese Insomnia Scale; ICD: International Classification of Diseases: NR: not reported.a
Range of years, ±only mentioned >20 years.

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p: We assessed the risk of bias of the 12 analytical observational studies and the 5 mendelian randomization studies. Regarding the first group, four studies were assessed as having a moderate risk of bias, and the domain of adequate description of the statistical analysis was the least fulfilled. Finally, four of the five mendelian randomization studies were assessed as having a low risk of bias (Supplementary material S4 & S5).

sec.title: Symptoms of insomnia and stroke
sec.p:
Among the 12 observational studies fulfilling our inclusion criteria, four were pooled. These studies included a total of 1,006,808 participants, of which 199,112 had symptoms of insomnia, and 807 696 made up the control group. In the pooled analysis, there was no significant association with the risk of developing all types of strokes between the two groups (HR: 1.16, 95% CI: 0.88-1.52, I2 = 60%). Regarding the subgroups, there was also no significant association with the risk of developing hemorrhagic stroke (HR: 1.00, 95% CI: 0.93-1.09, I2 = 56%), but there was a higher risk of developing ischemic stroke in the group with insomnia symptoms with no heterogeneity between studies (HR: 1.07, 95% CI: 1.04-1.10, I2 = 60%) (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
Hazard ratio of association between insomnia symptoms and risk of stroke in analytical observational studies. A) All types of strokes; B) Ischemic stroke; C) Hemorrhagic stroke.Fig. 1

Four other studies were also pooled, but in these the measure of association was OR. These studied included a total of 60,617 participants, 5773 of whom had insomnia symptoms and 54,844 constituted the control group. In this analysis, the group with insomnia symptoms showed a higher risk of developing all types of strokes (OR: 1.40, 95% CI: 1.11-1.77, I2 = 0%) and ischemic stroke (OR: 1.76, 95% CI: 1.17-2.66, I2 = 0%) than the control group (Fig. 2).Fig. 2
Odds ratio of association between insomnia symptoms and risk of stroke in observational studies. A) All types of strokes; B) Ischemic stroke.Fig. 2

The mendelian randomization analyses showed no significant association between the insomnia symptoms group and the risk of all types of strokes (OR: 1.09, 95% CI: 0.77-1.56, I2 = 73%). However, in comparison to the control group, a significant association was observed for ischemic stroke (OR: 1.13, 95% CI: 1.00-1.28, I2 = 72%), with a high level of heterogeneity between studies (Fig. 3).Fig. 3
Odds ratio of association between insomnia symptoms and risk of stroke in mendelian randomization studies. A) All types of strokes; B) Ischemic stroke.Fig. 3

sec.title: Diagnosis of insomnia and stroke
sec.p: A narrative synthesis of analytical observational studies suggests an association between the diagnosis of insomnia and an increased risk of stroke. Wu (2014) reported a significantly elevated risk for all types of strokes (HR: 1.55, 95% CI: 1.35-1.79) which was in accordance with the results of Kalmbach (2016) (OR: 3.79, 95% CI:1.94-7.42). For ischemic stroke, Park (2023) reported a significant association with the insomnia group (HR: 1.45, 95% CI:1.32-1.61), while Maalouf (2023) found a more modest association (OR: 1.04, 95% CI:1.00-1.08) (Supplementary Material S6). Regarding mendelian randomization studies, Sun (2021) reported a significant association between the diagnosis of insomnia and intracranial aneurysm (OR: 1.15, 95% CI:1.08-1.23), but there was no difference for the risk of developing intracerebral hemorrhage (OR: 1.00, 95% CI: 0.99-1.01) (Supplementary Material S7).

sec.title: Certainty of the evidence
sec.p:
We used GRADE for dichotomic outcomes between the insomnia symptoms group and stroke (Table 2). Since cohorts are the best type of study to evaluate the impact of exposure, we initiated the analysis with a high certainty of evidence but downgrade one level because the inconsistency I2 was >40%.Table 2
GRADE summary of findings between the insomnia symptoms group and stroke.Table 2
Outcome№ of participants (studies)Certainty of the evidence (GRADE)Anticipated absolute effects
Effect size95% CI
Insomnia symptoms
All types of strokes1,006,808 (4 cohort studies)⨁⨁⨁◯ModerateaHR: 1.16 (0.88-1.52)
Ischemic stroke487,200 (2 cohort studies)⨁⨁⨁⨁HighHR: 1.07 (1.04-1.10)
Hemorrhagic stroke487,200 (2 cohort studies)⨁⨁⨁◯ModerateaHR: 1.00 (0.93-1.09)
Diagnosis of insomnia
All types of stroke89,663 (1 cohort and 1 cross-sectional study)⨁⨁⨁◯ModeratebHR: 1.55 (1.35-1.79)
Ischemic stroke6,558,291 (1 cohort and 1 case-control study)⨁⨁⨁◯ModeratebHR: 1.45 (1.32-1.61)
CI: confidence interval; HR: hazard ratio.
GRADE Working Group grades of evidence.
High certainty: we are very confident that the true effect lies close to that of the estimate of the effect.
Moderate certainty: we are moderately confident in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be close to the estimate of the effect, but there is a possibility that it is substantially different.
Low certainty: our confidence in the effect estimate is limited: the true effect may be substantially different from the estimate of the effect.
Very low certainty: we have very little confidence in the effect estimate: the true effect is likely to be substantially different from the estimate of effect.
Explanations.a
Inconsistency due to heterogeneity I2 > 40%.b
Risk of bias due to 50% of the studies have Newcastle Ottawa Scale (NOS) ≥7.

sec.title: Summary of findings
sec.p: This systematic review with meta-analysis (7,696,920 participants) found that insomnia symptoms increased the risk of developing ischemic stroke with a high certainty of the evidence, while the diagnosis of insomnia was significantly associated with all types of strokes and ischemic stroke with moderate certainty of the evidence.

sec.title: Comparison with other systematic reviews
sec.p: Although our results did not show a significant association between the insomnia symptoms and all types of strokes, a higher risk of developing ischemic stroke was observed in the insomnia group which may be because isolated symptoms are not taken as a serious problem and, therefore, are not addressed or diagnosed [22]. These results are consistent with the findings of Usama et al. who reported that insomnia increased the risk of stroke 1.47-fold [23].

Although insomnia involves not only altered sleep duration but also altered sleep quality, patients with this diagnosis are usually more likely to sleep fewer hours which, according with Li et al. [24] increase 1.07 folds the risk of stroke for each 1-h shorter sleep duration. However, one systematic review found that sleeping more hours (>7 h) showed a higher risk to developing all types of stroke possibly due to underlying OSA or central disorders of hypersomnia [25], which seems to indicate that extreme sleep disturbances could be considered the main exposure factor. In addition, sleep duration is not only associated with this vascular disease but also predisposes individuals to a higher risk of mortality, which coincides with the meta-analysis of Yin et al. [26] which found that the risk of all-cause mortality significantly increased per every 1 h less than the usual 7 h of sleep (RR: 1.05, 95% CI: 1.01-1.09).

Regarding each type of stroke, we found an association between symptoms of insomnia and ischemic stroke, but not with hemorrhagic stroke, which partially coincides with another systematic review which found no association with any type of stroke, but did find that a long sleep duration was associated with ischemic stroke (HR: 1.14, 95% CI: 1.03-1.24). Furthermore, this same study also performed a meta-analysis of mendelian randomization studies to evaluate the same outcome. However, with this design, the results did not find an association between sleep disturbances and all types of stroke (OR:1.13, 95% CI: 1.00-1.27) or ischemic stroke (OR:1.10, 95% CI: 0.97-1.26) [27], which coincides with our results.

sec.title: Recommendations for future research
sec.p: The scientific literature has shown heterogeneity among observational studies, which could be explained by the imprecise measurement of insomnia symptoms. Taking into account that most studies use a form completed by the patients themselves - which is not objective information - the results would likely be more consistent if insomnia were measured by actigraphy, polysomnography or portable electroencephalography. Although several studies have determined the association between insomnia symptoms and stroke, there is limited information about the impact of this sleep disturbance on specific comorbidities in stroke survivors. For example, this cerebrovascular disease generates an inflammatory response with oxidative stress which causes death of adjacent neurons and, consequently, neurodegenerative diseases. In this sense, Liang et al. found that the risk of cognitive impairment is significantly associated with stroke survivors (OR: 2.91, 95% CI: 1.56-5.43), but this ratio was even higher if stroke survivors had symptoms of insomnia (OR: 4.16, 95% CI: 1.91-9.07) [28] thus it would be beneficial to evaluate whether preventing sleep disturbances could reduce the neurodegeneration of the survivor group. In addition, recent studies using electroencephalogram-based sleep metrics have shown that poor sleep quality, another characteristic of insomnia diagnosed, is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular, psychiatric, cognitive diseases and stroke [29] while healthy sleep patterns defined by multiple sleep behaviors including the absence of insomnia symptoms, reduced the risk of stroke and cardiovascular disease [30]. Thus, objective sleep measures based on neurophysiological patterns may help reinforce the biological plausibility of this association and further research is needed to disentangle the independent contribution of insomnia symptoms.

Different types of studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of insomnia treatment involving cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) in decreasing the severity of insomnia, improving sleep quality and reducing fatigue, anxiety or depression [31,32]. Nevertheless, there is insufficient information about the effect of CBT on stroke prevention, making the development of clinical trials necessary. Regarding other aspects, the studies included in the present review included different age groups which could alter the true effect of risk since people <50 years of age presented a greater risk of developing stroke (HR: 3.84, 95% CI: 1.50-9.85) than those ≥50 years old (HR: 1.38, 95% CI: 1.18-1.62) [33]. Therefore, subgroup analyses not only by age but also by menopausal status is necessary because some studies have reported that postmenopausal women with a sleep duration of ≥9 h have a high risk of developing ischemic stroke (RR: 1.70, 95% CI: 1.32-2.21) [34] due to the reduction of estrogen, progesterone, and melatonin which disrupts thermoregulation, circadian rhythm, and sleep quality [35].

Finally, although there were no restrictions regarding language, none of our studies were from Latin America. This is relevant because the literature has shown a relationship between a low socioeconomic level, such as in the Latin American population, and a high prevalence of sleep disturbances [36]. Indeed, Brazil and Mexico are among the 10 countries with the highest number of cases of insomnia disorder [37]. Therefore, studies focused on low-income countries are necessary to identify and control the disparities that decrease the quality of life of people with sleep disorders.

sec.title: Clinical applicability
sec.p: In this study, different types of questionnaires were used to determine the association between insomnia and the risk of stroke. Similar to previous studies which reported a high sensitivity and moderate specificity of the use of questionnaires, our findings suggest that although questionnaires cannot replace objective measurements, such as actigraphy, they may serve as effective, low-cost, and easily applicable screening tools in large populations or resource-limited settings to identify individuals who require further evaluation [38,39].

In addition, there is strong evidence about the impact of insomnia not only from a clinical but also economic point of view, whose costs are 60% higher than those without insomnia. The largest amount (77 to 92 billion dollars) allocated to indirect costs like comorbidities, hospitalization, alcohol abuse, motor vehicle crashes and work difficulties [40].

Regarding the results of the mendelian randomization studies, these reinforce the causal relationship between insomnia and stroke, specifically the ischemic type, and thus, this sleep disturbance could be considered as an early indicator of stroke and be included in cardiovascular risk predictor scales since the presentation of even one insomnia symptom is associated with a 1.16-fold increased risk of cardiovascular disease events [22]. Although symptoms or diagnosed diseases are not usually included in prediction tools, there are scenarios, such as the Erasmus Guillain-Barre Syndrome Outcome Score (EGOS) to predict functional outcomes in patients with this syndrome, in which one of the variables takes into account is preexisting diarrhea [41]. Similarly, the robust Dementia Risk Score (DRS) includes sleep disturbance to predict the four-year risk of developing dementia; however, this tool has not yet been externally validated [42].

sec.title: Limitations and strengths
sec.p: This systematic review has some limitations. First, although we carried out an exhaustive search of the literature, we may not have found all the articles. Second, there was heterogeneity between the measure of insomnia symptoms and their definition, which could affect the real identification of exposed patients. Third, most of the studies were from high- and middle-income countries, and therefore these results cannot be generalized. Finally, the included studies did not report specific insomnia symptoms separately, such as sleep maintenance insomnia or non-restorative sleep, so we were unable to examine these symptoms individually or explore their potential association with obstructive sleep apnea. Regarding the strengths of our review, it is the first systematic review with meta-analysis about insomnia symptoms and stroke. In addition, we also included Mendelian randomization and case-control studies to support the results of the cohort studies; however further longitudinal and interventional studies are needed to determine causality. Likewise, we evaluated the certainty of our results with the GRADE approach.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Our results did not show a significant association between insomnia symptoms and all types of strokes; however, a higher risk of developing ischemic stroke was observed in the group with insomnia symptoms. In contrast, reporting insomnia based on diagnostic criteria was significantly associated with all types of strokes. Despite these findings, there was heterogeneity among studies due to the use of self-reported questionnaires; therefore, future studies should employ validated tools to obtain more consistent results and assess the contribution of isolated symptoms to stroke risk. Moreover, research conducted in low socioeconomic settings is necessary to identify disparities that reduce the quality of life of individuals with sleep disorders.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Miguel Cabanillas-Lazo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Milagros Pascual-Guevara: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Gustavo Tapia-Sequeiros: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation. Adan V. Sifuentes-Montalvo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Data curation.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

sec.title: Funding sources
sec.p: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors have nothing to report.

fn.label: Appendix A
fn.p: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sleepx.2026.100177.
